I caught sight of her at the play and in answer to her beckoning1 I went over during the interval and sat down beside her. It was long since I had last seen her and if someone had not mentioned her name I do not think I would have recognised her. She addressed me brightly.“Well, it’s many years since we first met. How time flies! We are not getting any younger. Do you remember the first time I saw you? You asked me to luncheon.” Did I remember?It was twenty years ago and I was living in Paris. I had a tiny apartment in the Latin Quarter and I was earning barely enough money to keep body and soul together. She had read a book of mine and had written to me about it. I answered, thanking 1. beckoning: calling or signaling her, and presently I received from her another letter saying someone with a gesturethat she was passing through Paris and would like to have a chat with me; but her time was limited and the only free moment she had was on the following Thursday. She asked me if I would give her a little luncheon at Foyot’s. Foyot’s is a restaurant at which the French senators eat and it was so far beyond my means that I had never even thought of going there. But I was flattered and I was too young to say no to a woman. I had eighty francs to live on till the end of the month and a modest2 2. modest: small and not luncheon should not cost expensive or excessive more than fifteen. If I cut out coffee for the next two weeks, I could manage well enough.I answered that I would meet her at Foyot’s on Thursday at half past twelve.She was not so young as I expected and in appearance imposing3 rather than attractive. She was in fact a woman of forty, and she gave me the impression of having more teeth, white and large and even, than were necessary for any practical purpose. She was talkative, but since she seemed inclined to talk about 3. imposing: impressive in appearance, me, I was prepared to be an attentive listener. I was often because of size or manner startled when the menu was brought, for the prices 4. reassured: made someone feel less were a great deal higher than I had expected. But she worried reassured4 me.“I never eat anything for luncheon,” she said. “Oh, don’t say that!” I answered generously5.“I never eat more than one thing. I think ? When the woman says she “never eats more people eat too much now-a-days. A little than one thing for luncheon”, why might the fish, perhaps. I wonder if they have any narrator feel relieved at this statement? salmon.”Well, it was early in the year for salmon and it was not on the menu, but I asked the waiter if there was any. Yes, they had a beautiful salmon, it was the first they had had. I ordered it for my guest. The waiter asked her if she would have something while it was being cooked.“No,” she answered, “I never eat more than one thing. Unless you have a little caviar6. I never mind caviar.”My heart sank a little. I knew I could not afford caviar, but I could not tell her that. I told the waiter by all means to bring caviar. For myself, I chose the cheapest dish on the menu and that was a mutton chop.generously: in a kind mannercaviar: the salted eggs of fish	“I think you’re unwise to eat meat,” she said. “I don’t know how you can expect to work after eating heavy things like chops. I never overload my stomach.”Then came the question of drink.“I never drink anything for luncheon,” she said. “Neither do I,” I answered promptly.“Except white wine,” she went on as though I had not spoken. “These French white wines are so light. They are wonderful for the digestion.” “What would you like?” I asked her.“My doctor won’t let me drink anything but champagne.” I think I turned a little pale. I ordered half a bottle. I mentioned casually that my doctor had absolutely forbidden me to drink champagne.“What are you going to drink, then?”“Water.”She ate the caviar and she ate the salmon. She talked gaily of art and literature and music. But I wondered what the bill would come to. When my mutton chop arrived she said:“I see that you’re in the habit of eating a heavy luncheon. I’m sure it’s a mistake. Why don’t you follow my example and just eat one thing? I’m sure you’d feel much better then.”“I am only going to eat one thing,” I said, as the waiter came again with the menu. She waved him aside with a light gesture.“No, no, I never eat anything for luncheon. Just a bite, I never want more than that. I can’t eat anything more unless they had some of those giant asparagus7. I should be sorry to leave Paris without having some of them.”	7. asparagus: a long green My heart sank. I had seen them in the shops and I knew that they vegetable eaten as food were horribly expensive. My mouth had often watered at the sight of them. “Madam wants to know if you have any of those giant asparagus,” I asked the waiter.I hoped he would say no. A happy smile spread over his broad face, and he assured me that they had some so large, so splendid, so tender, that it was a marvel.“I’m not in the least hungry,” my guest sighed, “but if you insist I don’t mind having some asparagus.” I ordered them.“Aren’t you going to have any?” “No, I never eat asparagus.”“I know there are people who don’t like them.”We waited for the asparagus to be cooked. Panic8 seized me. It was not a question now how much money I should have left for the rest of the month, but whether I had enough to pay the bill. It would be terrible to find myself ten francs short and be obliged to borrow from my guest. I could not bring myself to do that. I knew exactly how much money I had and if the bill came to more, I made up my mind that I would put 8. panic: sudden fear or anxiety my hand in my pocket and with a dramatic cry start up and say my money had been stolen. If she had not money enough to pay the bill then the only thing to do would be to leave my watch and say I would come back and pay later.The asparagus appeared. They were enormous and appetizing9. The smell of the melted butter tickled my nostrils. I watched the woman send them down her throat and in my polite way I talked on the condition of the drama in the Balkans. At 9. appetizing: looking or last she finished.	smelling good enough to eatThe India BookmarkIn traditional Indian culture, meals were often served on banana leaves. Scientists have found that banana leaves contain natural plant compounds that can act as mild antioxidants and are biodegradable“Coffee?” I said.	? When the woman advises the narrator to eat lightly, why is this advice ironic “Yes, just an ice-cream and coffee,” she in the context of the story?answered.It was all the same to me now, so I ordered coffee for myself and an ice-cream and coffee you.”in season then. Lord knew what they cost. I knew too — a little later, for my guest, going on with her conversation, absent-mindedly took one. “You see, you’ve filled your stomach with a lot of meat and you can’t eat any more. But I’ve just had a snack and I shall enjoy a peach.”The bill came and when I paid it I found that I had only enough for a quite inadequate10 tip. Her eyes rested for a moment on the three francs I left for the waiter and I knew that she thought me mean. But when I walked out of the restaurant I had the 10. inadequate: not whole month before me and not a penny in my pocket. enough; insufficient “Follow my example,” she said as we shook hands, “and never eat more than one thing for luncheon.”“I’ll do better than that,” I answered. “I’ll eat nothing for dinner tonight.”“Humorist!11” she cried gaily, jumping into a cab. “You’re ? When the woman advises quite a humorist!” But I have had my revenge at last. the narrator to eat lightly, Today she weighs twenty-	why is this advice ironic in 11. humorist: a person who is one stone. amusing or writes humorously the context of the story?Where does the narrator meet the woman for lunch?London 	(b) Paris 	(c) Rome 	(d) BerlinWhich expensive dish does the woman order first?Fish 	(b) Salmon 	(c) Caviar 	(d) ChickenWhat does the woman claim about her eating habits?She eats a lot 	(b) She eats only fruits   	(c) She never eats lunch 	(d) She eats very little  	4. Why does the narrator eat very little during the lunch? 	(a) He is not hungry 	(b) He dislikes the food 	(c) He cannot afford much 	(d) He is sick 	5. What advice does the woman repeatedly give the narrator? 	(a) To eat more 	(b) To eat lightly  	(c) To drink more water 	(d) To order dessertsFill in the Blanks The narrator accepted the invitation despite limited funds because he wanted to appear ________________.The woman’s statement “I never eat more than one thing” is ________________.The narrator avoids ordering expensive food mainly due to his ________________.The ending creates irony because the woman later becomes ________________.The woman orders multiple dishes despite claiming to eat very ________________.Arrange the following events in the order  	(1-6) in which they happened in the chapter. 	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingThe woman confidently orders expensive dishes like asparagus, even though  she claims to eat very little. Many years later, the narrator recalls the incident with irony, noting that the  woman has become very fat. The narrator grows increasingly anxious as the bill rises, while he himself eats  very little. The woman keeps advising the narrator to eat lightly, even as she continues  ordering more costly items. At the end of the meal, the narrator pays the bill and is left with almost no  money. The narrator, a young writer with limited money, agrees to take the woman  to an expensive restaurant in Paris. Recall the sequence of major events before numbering them.Answer the following questions: 	/Did the woman really eat very little? Give examples.What change came in the narrator’s feelings during the lunch and why?What did the woman repeatedly advise the narrator? What does it tell you about her?“The narrator suffered silently throughout the meal.” Explain this statement with reference to the story.How did the narrator later look back at this incident?How did the lunch finally end for the narrator?	Put on Your Thinking Caps	 	/Choose two adjectives from each list that could be used to describe the noun.dress – slow, frayed, tired, tornvoyage – perilous, howling, long, prettyholiday – exciting, short, faithful, deepflowers – odious, heartfelt, fragrant, goldenstorm – overpowering, tranquil, raging, healthystory – thrilling, friendly, enjoyable, warmdialogues – witty, irritating, low, humorousmeal – hasty, fast, economical, sumptuous	Dictionary & Vocabulary Skills	  	/Some words may appear to have similar meanings, but each has a distinct meaning and usage. Use a dictionary to find the meanings of the words polite, courteous, considerate, and respectful. Then read the descriptions below and write the most suitable word.A person who thinks about the feelings and comfort of others:  __________________A person who shows good manners in social situations:  	__________________  	3. A person who treats others with admiration and regard:  	__________________  	4. A person who behaves in a well-mannered and civil way:  	__________________Choose words from the box to complete the following statements about people’s professions. You may refer to a dictionary.	Word Box	lexicographer, psychologist, novelist, almoner, shipwright, stevedore, taxidermist, chandlerA __________ writes novels.A __________ makes or sells candles.	Check each The one who builds ships is called a __________.	profession’s A __________ compiles a dictionary.	meaning before choosing the A __________ stuffs the skins of dead animals to give them a correct word. lifelike appearance.The one who studies the working of a human mind is called a __________.The person who is in-charge of giving assistance to the poor in places like a hospital is called a __________.A __________ helps to load or unload ships.	Grammar Lab	  	/Grammar LabPhrases and ClausesA phrase is a group of words that does not have a subject and a verb together, and does not express a complete idea. 	 It adds meaning, but cannot stand alone. 	 Examples: in a difficult situation 	 	with great courage  	 These groups of words give extra information but are incomplete.A clause is a group of words that has a subject and a verb. 	 It may or may not express a complete idea.Types of Clauses 	1. Main Clause  	2. Subordinate Clause 	 •  Has a subject and a verb  •  Has a subject and a verb 	 •  Expresses a complete thought  •  Does not express a complete thought 	 •  Can stand alone  •  Depends on the main clause 	 Example:  Example: 	 •  She made the right choice.  • when the teacher explained the problemWrite whether the highlighted part is a phrase or a clause.She acted wisely in a difficult situation.He remained calm when the problem became serious.They worked hard with full dedication.She succeeded because she practised regularly.He spoke confidently in front of everyone.They continued their work although they were tired.ConnectCommunicate	Listening Skills	 	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingFill in the missing words to complete the conversation after listening to the passage read by your teacher.Host: Please order whatever you ____________.Guest: Thank you. I will choose only a ____________ meal.Host: That's very ____________ of you.Guest: Taking more food than necessary often leads to ____________.	Speaking Skills	 	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingConduct a short survey to understand the food preferences of students between 10 and 16 years of age. Each student will interview five classmates or schoolmates. Ask questions such as:What is your favourite food?How often do you eat junk food?Do you prefer home-cooked food or packaged food?Which healthy food item do you enjoy the most?What change would you like to make in your eating habits?After completing the interviews, write a brief summary of your findings. Use comparison words such as most, many, a few, very few, hardly any, majority, minority etc.Now form groups of five. Combine your individual findings, prepare a short oral presentation, and share your group’s observations with the class.	Writing Skills	  	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingWrite a review of a book or a story. You may take about three to four weeks to complete this project.Choose a story/storybook that interests you.Enjoy reading the story at leisure and make short notes alongside. You could do so every time you have read a certain part of the story.Write a summary of the story.Include the following in the review:the title, 	• the author’s name,         • the theme of the story,your opinion of the story and the writer’s style of writing, characters and anything else of special mention about. Competencies BeyondCG:2, C:2.2	 Interconnected Learning with Hindi  	Critical ThinkingWrite the Hindi meanings of the following English words. Then use these words in your own sentences.guest 	- _____________ -  ____________________________________________expensive - _____________ -  ____________________________________________polite 	- _____________ -  ____________________________________________  • choice 	- _____________ -  ____________________________________________hunger - _____________ -  ____________________________________________  SEL Corner  Healthy Relationships CG:2, C:2.2 Critical Thinking ῀Complete the sentences using suitable words from the help box about good manners at a restaurant.	Word Box	thank you   table   utensils   phone   served   passWhen eating at a restaurant, keep your elbows off the __________.It is polite to wait until everyone is __________ before starting your meal.Use __________ properly, starting with the outermost fork or spoon.If you need something that is not near you, politely ask someone to __________ it.Avoid using your __________ at the table to show respect.Remember to say ‘please’ and ‘__________’ to the restaurant staff.	 Fun & Creativity 	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingImagine yourself as a restaurateur. Choose any ten food items that you will keep in your restaurant. Fix a price for each food item. Create a menu card in the space provided below. 1. ____________________ 2. ____________________  3. _____________________  4. ____________________ 5. ____________________  6. _____________________ 7. ____________________ 8. ____________________  9. _____________________   Now, as a customer, choose any five food items that you will eat. 1. _____________ 2. ___________  3. ___________ 4. __________5. ___________ Add up the prices of your chosen food items.
